questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a 'Positioning Statement'?

options.text: To serve as a catchy slogan for your homepage
To be an internal 'North Star' that keeps strategy focused
To be memorized and recited to investors
To help SEO crawlers index your site

questions.explanation: A positioning statement is primarily an internal tool. It defines who you are and who you serve so you can make consistent decisions about product, marketing, and sales.

questions.question: Which of these is the standard formula for a positioning statement?

options.text: We are the Uber for [Industry].
For [Target] Who [Need], Our Product is a [Category] That [Benefit]. Unlike [Alternative], We [Differentiator].
The details don't matter as long as it sounds professional.
Just list your top 5 features and add a price.

questions.explanation: This specific formula (from 'Crossing the Chasm') forces you to identify all the critical components: Target, Need, Category, Benefit, Alternative, and Differentiator.

questions.question: What is the 'So What?' test?

options.text: A psychological test for potential hires
Asking 'So what?' repeatedly until you reach a tangible business outcome (money, time, risk)
A way to dismiss competitor claims
Asking customers if they care about your features

questions.explanation: The 'So What?' test forces you to move beyond features ('it uses AI') to benefits ('it's faster') to outcomes ('you won't lose customers').

questions.question: How should your positioning differ for a 'Website Headline' vs. an 'Internal Statement'?

options.text: It shouldn't; they should be identical.
The website headline should be shorter (under 10 words) and focused purely on the immediate outcome.
The website headline should be longer to explain everything upfront.
The internal statement should be vaguer to allow flexibility.

questions.explanation: Context matters. An internal statement provides complete context. A website headline has about 3 seconds to grab attention, so it must be short and outcome-driven.

questions.question: Why does using passive voice weaken a positioning statement?

options.text: It implies a lack of ownership and authority.
It is grammatically incorrect.
It makes the sentence too short.
Passive voice is actually preferred in B2B.

questions.explanation: Active voice ('We help you...') is strong and authoritative. Passive voice ('Solutions are provided by us...') is weak and distant. Positioning requires confidence.

questions.question: Draft your 'Website Headline' version of your positioning. Keep it under 10 words. It should answer: 'What do I get out of this?'
questions.promptForAI: Evaluate the student's headline. Is it under 10 words? Is it outcome-focused or feature-focused? Suggest a stronger, punchier variation.
